translationText: Numbered Discourses 3.65
7. The Great Chapter
With the Kālāmas of Kesamutta
So I have heard.

rootText: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kosalesu cārikaṁ caramāno mahatā bhikkhusaṅghena saddhiṁ yena kesamuttaṁ nāma kālāmānaṁ nigamo tadavasari.
translationText: At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a town of the Kālāmas named Kesamutta.
The Kālāmas of Kesamutta heard:

rootText: “samaṇo khalu, bho, gotamo sakyaputto sakyakulā pabbajito kesamuttaṁ anuppatto.
translationText: “It seems the ascetic Gotama—a Sakyan, gone forth from a Sakyan family—has arrived at Kesamutta.

rootText: Taṁ kho pana bhavantaṁ gotamaṁ evaṁ kalyāṇo kittisaddo abbhuggato:
translationText: He has this good reputation:
‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha …’

rootText: sādhu kho pana tathārūpānaṁ arahataṁ dassanaṁ hotī’”ti.
translationText: It’s good to see such perfected ones.”

rootText: Atha kho kesamuttiyā kālāmā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā appekacce bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu, appekacce bhagavatā saddhiṁ sammodiṁsu, sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu, appekacce yena bhagavā tenañjaliṁ paṇāmetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu, appekacce nāmagottaṁ sāvetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu, appekacce tuṇhībhūtā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu. Ekamantaṁ nisinnā kho te kesamuttiyā kālāmā bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
translationText: Then the Kālāmas went up to the Buddha. Before sitting down to one side, some bowed, some exchanged greetings and polite conversation, some held up their cupped palms toward the Buddha, some announced their name and clan, while some kept silent. Seated to one side the Kālāmas said to the Buddha:
“There are, sir, some ascetics and brahmins who come to Kesamutta.

rootText: Te sakaṁyeva vādaṁ dīpenti jotenti, parappavādaṁ pana khuṁsenti vambhenti paribhavanti omakkhiṁ karonti.
translationText: They explain and promote only their own doctrine, while they attack, badmouth, disparage, and smear the doctrines of others.

rootText: Aparepi, bhante, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kesamuttaṁ āgacchanti.
translationText: Then some other ascetics and brahmins come to Kesamutta.

rootText: Tepi sakaṁyeva vādaṁ dīpenti jotenti, parappavādaṁ pana khuṁsenti vambhenti paribhavanti omakkhiṁ karonti.
translationText: They too explain and promote only their own doctrine, while they attack, badmouth, disparage, and smear the doctrines of others.

rootText: Tesaṁ no, bhante, amhākaṁ hoteva kaṅkhā hoti vicikicchā:
translationText: So, sir, we’re doubting and uncertain:

rootText: ‘ko su nāma imesaṁ bhavataṁ samaṇabrāhmaṇānaṁ saccaṁ āha, ko musā’”ti?
translationText: ‘I wonder who of these respected ascetics and brahmins speaks the truth, and who speaks falsehood?’”
“It is enough, Kālāmas, for you to be doubting and uncertain.

rootText: Kaṅkhanīyeva pana vo ṭhāne vicikicchā uppannā.
translationText: Doubt has come up in you about an uncertain matter.

rootText: Etha tumhe, kālāmā, mā anussavena, mā paramparāya, mā itikirāya, mā piṭakasampadānena, mā takkahetu, mā nayahetu, mā ākāraparivitakkena, mā diṭṭhinijjhānakkhantiyā, mā bhabbarūpatāya, mā samaṇo no garūti.
translationText: Please, Kālāmas, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think ‘The ascetic is our respected teacher.’
But when you know for yourselves:

rootText: ‘ime dhammā akusalā, ime dhammā sāvajjā, ime dhammā viññugarahitā, ime dhammā samattā samādinnā ahitāya dukkhāya saṁvattantī’ti, atha tumhe, kālāmā, pajaheyyātha.
translationText: ‘These things are unskillful, blameworthy, criticized by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering’, then you should give them up.
What do you think, Kālāmas?

rootText: lobho purisassa ajjhattaṁ uppajjamāno uppajjati hitāya vā ahitāya vā”ti?
translationText: Does greed come up in a person for their welfare or harm?”
“Harm, sir.”

rootText: “Luddho panāyaṁ, kālāmā, purisapuggalo lobhena abhibhūto pariyādinnacitto pāṇampi hanati, adinnampi ādiyati, paradārampi gacchati, musāpi bhaṇati, parampi tathattāya samādapeti, yaṁ sa hoti dīgharattaṁ ahitāya dukkhāyā”ti.
translationText: “A greedy individual, overcome by greed, kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?”
“Yes, sir.”
“What do you think, Kālāmas?

rootText: doso purisassa ajjhattaṁ uppajjamāno uppajjati hitāya vā ahitāya vā”ti?
translationText: Does hate come up in a person for their welfare or harm?”
“Harm, sir.”

rootText: “Duṭṭho panāyaṁ, kālāmā, purisapuggalo dosena abhibhūto pariyādinnacitto pāṇampi hanati, adinnampi ādiyati, paradārampi gacchati, musāpi bhaṇati, parampi tathattāya samādapeti, yaṁ sa hoti dīgharattaṁ ahitāya dukkhāyā”ti.
translationText: “A hateful individual, overcome by hate, kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?”
“Yes, sir.”
“What do you think, Kālāmas?

rootText: moho purisassa ajjhattaṁ uppajjamāno uppajjati hitāya vā ahitāya vā”ti?
translationText: Does delusion come up in a person for their welfare or harm?”
“Harm, sir.”

rootText: “Mūḷho panāyaṁ, kālāmā, purisapuggalo mohena abhibhūto pariyādinnacitto pāṇampi hanati, adinnampi ādiyati, paradārampi gacchati, musāpi bhaṇati, parampi tathattāya samādapeti, yaṁ sa hoti dīgharattaṁ ahitāya dukkhāyā”ti.
translationText: “A deluded individual, overcome by delusion, kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?”
“Yes, sir.”

rootText: “Taṁ kiṁ maññatha, kālāmā, ime dhammā kusalā vā akusalā vā”ti?
translationText: “What do you think, Kālāmas, are these things skillful or unskillful?”
“Unskillful, sir.”
“Blameworthy or blameless?”
“Blameworthy, sir.”
“Criticized or praised by sensible people?”
“Criticized by sensible people, sir.”

rootText: “Samattā samādinnā ahitāya dukkhāya saṁvattanti, no vā?
translationText: “When you undertake them, do they lead to harm and suffering, or not?
Or how do you see this?”
“When you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering.
That’s how we see it.”
“So, Kālāmas, when I said:
‘Please,

rootText: Mā anussavena, mā paramparāya, mā itikirāya, mā piṭakasampadānena, mā takkahetu, mā nayahetu, mā ākāraparivitakkena, mā diṭṭhinijjhānakkhantiyā, mā bhabbarūpatāya, mā samaṇo no garūti.
translationText: don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think “The ascetic is our respected teacher.”
But when you know for yourselves:

rootText: “ime dhammā akusalā, ime dhammā sāvajjā, ime dhammā viññugarahitā, ime dhammā samattā samādinnā ahitāya dukkhāya saṁvattantīti, atha tumhe, kālāmā, pajaheyyāthā”’ti,
translationText: “These things are unskillful, blameworthy, criticized by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering”, then you should give them up.’

rootText: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
translationText: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.

rootText: Etha tumhe, kālāmā, mā anussavena, mā paramparāya, mā itikirāya, mā piṭakasampadānena, mā takkahetu, mā nayahetu, mā ākāraparivitakkena, mā diṭṭhinijjhānakkhantiyā, mā bhabbarūpatāya, mā samaṇo no garūti.
translationText: Please, Kālāmas, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think ‘The ascetic is our respected teacher.’
But when you know for yourselves:

rootText: ‘ime dhammā kusalā, ime dhammā anavajjā, ime dhammā viññuppasatthā, ime dhammā samattā samādinnā hitāya sukhāya saṁvattantī’ti, atha tumhe, kālāmā, upasampajja vihareyyātha.
translationText: ‘These things are skillful, blameless, praised by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness’, then you should acquire them and keep them.
What do you think, Kālāmas?

rootText: alobho purisassa ajjhattaṁ uppajjamāno uppajjati hitāya vā ahitāya vā”ti?
translationText: Does contentment come up in a person for their welfare or harm?”
“Welfare, sir.”

rootText: “Aluddho panāyaṁ, kālāmā, purisapuggalo lobhena anabhibhūto apariyādinnacitto neva pāṇaṁ hanati, na adinnaṁ ādiyati, na paradāraṁ gacchati, na musā bhaṇati, na parampi tathattāya samādapeti, yaṁ sa hoti dīgharattaṁ hitāya sukhāyā”ti.
translationText: “An individual who is content, not overcome by greed, doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit adultery, lie, or encourage others to do the same. Is that for their lasting welfare and happiness?”
“Yes, sir.”
“What do you think, Kālāmas?
Does love come up in a person for their welfare or harm? …
Does understanding come up in a person for their welfare or harm? …
Is that for their lasting welfare and happiness?”
“Yes, sir.”

rootText: “Taṁ kiṁ maññatha, kālāmā, ime dhammā kusalā vā akusalā vā”ti?
translationText: “What do you think, Kālāmas, are these things skillful or unskillful?”
“Skillful, sir.”
“Blameworthy or blameless?”
“Blameless, sir.”
“Criticized or praised by sensible people?”
“Praised by sensible people, sir.”

rootText: “Samattā samādinnā hitāya sukhāya saṁvattanti no vā?
translationText: “When you undertake them, do they lead to welfare and happiness, or not?
Or how do you see this?”
“When you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness.
That’s how we see it.”
“So, Kālāmas, when I said:
‘Please,

rootText: Mā anussavena, mā paramparāya, mā itikirāya, mā piṭakasampadānena, mā takkahetu, mā nayahetu, mā ākāraparivitakkena, mā diṭṭhinijjhānakkhantiyā, mā bhabbarūpatāya, mā samaṇo no garūti.
translationText: don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think “The ascetic is our respected teacher.”
But when you know for yourselves:

rootText: ime dhammā kusalā, ime dhammā anavajjā, ime dhammā viññuppasatthā, ime dhammā samattā samādinnā hitāya sukhāya saṁvattantīti, atha tumhe, kālāmā, upasampajja vihareyyāthā’ti,
translationText: “These things are skillful, blameless, praised by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness”, then you should acquire them and keep them.’

rootText: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
translationText: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.

rootText: Sa kho so, kālāmā, ariyasāvako evaṁ vigatābhijjho vigatabyāpādo asammūḷho sampajāno patissato mettāsahagatena cetasā ekaṁ disaṁ pharitvā viharati, tathā dutiyaṁ, tathā tatiyaṁ, tathā catutthaṁ, iti uddhamadho tiriyaṁ sabbadhi sabbattatāya sabbāvantaṁ lokaṁ mettāsahagatena cetasā vipulena mahaggatena appamāṇena averena abyāpajjhena pharitvā viharati.
translationText: Then that noble disciple is rid of desire, rid of ill will, unconfused, aware, and mindful. They meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.

They meditate spreading a heart full of compassion to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of compassion to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.

They meditate spreading a heart full of rejoicing to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of rejoicing to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.

rootText: upekkhāsahagatena cetasā ekaṁ disaṁ pharitvā viharati, tathā dutiyaṁ, tathā tatiyaṁ, tathā catutthaṁ, iti uddhamadho tiriyaṁ sabbadhi sabbattatāya sabbāvantaṁ lokaṁ upekkhāsahagatena cetasā vipulena mahaggatena appamāṇena averena abyāpajjhena pharitvā viharati.
translationText: They meditate spreading a heart full of equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.

rootText: Sa kho so, kālāmā, ariyasāvako evaṁ averacitto evaṁ abyāpajjhacitto evaṁ asaṅkiliṭṭhacitto evaṁ visuddhacitto.
translationText: When that noble disciple has a mind that’s free of enmity and ill will, uncorrupted and purified,

rootText: Tassa diṭṭheva dhamme cattāro assāsā adhigatā honti.
translationText: they’ve won four consolations in this very life.

rootText: ‘Sace kho pana atthi paro loko, atthi sukatadukkaṭānaṁ kammānaṁ phalaṁ vipāko, athāhaṁ kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā sugatiṁ saggaṁ lokaṁ upapajjissāmī’ti, ayamassa paṭhamo assāso adhigato hoti.
translationText: ‘If it turns out there is another world, and good and bad deeds have a result, then—when the body breaks up, after death—I’ll be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ This is the first consolation they’ve won.

rootText: ‘Sace kho pana natthi paro loko, natthi sukatadukkaṭānaṁ kammānaṁ phalaṁ vipāko, athāhaṁ diṭṭheva dhamme averaṁ abyāpajjhaṁ anīghaṁ sukhiṁ attānaṁ pariharāmī’ti, ayamassa dutiyo assāso adhigato hoti.
translationText: ‘If it turns out there is no other world, and good and bad deeds don’t have a result, then in this very life I’ll keep myself free of enmity and ill will, untroubled and happy.’ This is the second consolation they’ve won.

rootText: ‘Sace kho pana karoto karīyati pāpaṁ, na kho panāhaṁ kassaci pāpaṁ cetemi.
translationText: ‘If it turns out that bad things happen to people who do bad things, then since I have no bad intentions,

rootText: Akarontaṁ kho pana maṁ pāpakammaṁ kuto dukkhaṁ phusissatī’ti, ayamassa tatiyo assāso adhigato hoti.
translationText: and since I’m not doing anything bad, how can suffering touch me?’ This is the third consolation they’ve won.

rootText: ‘Sace kho pana karoto na karīyati pāpaṁ, athāhaṁ ubhayeneva visuddhaṁ attānaṁ samanupassāmī’ti, ayamassa catuttho assāso adhigato hoti.
translationText: ‘If it turns out that bad things don’t happen to people who do bad things, then I still see myself pure on both sides.’ This is the fourth consolation they’ve won.

rootText: Sa kho so, kālāmā, ariyasāvako evaṁ averacitto evaṁ abyāpajjhacitto evaṁ asaṅkiliṭṭhacitto evaṁ visuddhacitto.
translationText: When that noble disciple has a mind that’s free of enmity and ill will, uncorrupted and purified,

rootText: Tassa diṭṭheva dhamme ime cattāro assāsā adhigatā hontī”ti.
translationText: they’ve won these four consolations in this very life.”
“That’s so true, Blessed One! That’s so true, Holy One!

rootText: Sa kho so, bhante, ariyasāvako evaṁ averacitto evaṁ abyāpajjhacitto evaṁ asaṅkiliṭṭhacitto evaṁ visuddhacitto.
translationText: When that noble disciple has a mind that’s free of enmity and ill will, uncorrupted and purified,

rootText: Tassa diṭṭheva dhamme cattāro assāsā adhigatā honti.
translationText: they’ve won these four consolations in this very life. …

rootText: ‘Sace kho pana atthi paro loko, atthi sukatadukkaṭānaṁ kammānaṁ phalaṁ vipāko, athāhaṁ kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā sugatiṁ saggaṁ lokaṁ upapajjissāmī’ti, ayamassa paṭhamo assāso adhigato hoti.
‘Sace kho pana natthi paro loko, natthi sukatadukkaṭānaṁ kammānaṁ phalaṁ vipāko, athāhaṁ diṭṭheva dhamme averaṁ abyāpajjhaṁ anīghaṁ sukhiṁ attānaṁ pariharāmī’ti, ayamassa dutiyo assāso adhigato hoti.
‘Sace kho pana karoto karīyati pāpaṁ, na kho panāhaṁ kassaci pāpaṁ cetemi, akarontaṁ kho pana maṁ pāpakammaṁ kuto dukkhaṁ phusissatī’ti, ayamassa tatiyo assāso adhigato hoti.
‘Sace kho pana karoto na karīyati pāpaṁ, athāhaṁ ubhayeneva visuddhaṁ attānaṁ samanupassāmī’ti, ayamassa catuttho assāso adhigato hoti.
Sa kho so, bhante, ariyasāvako evaṁ averacitto evaṁ abyāpajjhacitto evaṁ asaṅkiliṭṭhacitto evaṁ visuddhacitto.
Tassa diṭṭheva dhamme ime cattāro assāsā adhigatā honti.

translationText: Excellent, sir! Excellent! …

rootText: ete mayaṁ, bhante, bhagavantaṁ saraṇaṁ gacchāma dhammañca bhikkhusaṅghañca.
translationText: We go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha.

rootText: Upāsake no, bhante, bhagavā dhāretu ajjatagge pāṇupete saraṇaṁ gate”ti.
translationText: From this day forth, may the Buddha remember us as lay followers who have gone for refuge for life.”

All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never leaves him.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"--in those who harbour such thoughts hatred will never cease.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"--in those who do not harbour such thoughts hatred will cease.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time: hatred ceases by love, this is an old rule.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The world does not know that we must all come to an end here;--but those who know it, their quarrels cease at once.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who lives looking for pleasures only, his senses uncontrolled, immoderate in his food, idle, and weak, Mara (the tempter) will certainly overthrow him, as the wind throws down a weak tree.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who lives without looking for pleasures, his senses well controlled, moderate in his food, faithful and strong, him Mara will certainly not overthrow, any more than the wind throws down a rocky mountain.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who wishes to put on the yellow dress without having cleansed himself from sin, who disregards temperance and truth, is unworthy of the yellow dress.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But he who has cleansed himself from sin, is well grounded in all virtues, and regards also temperance and truth, he is indeed worthy of the yellow dress.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: They who imagine truth in untruth, and see untruth in truth, never arrive at truth, but follow vain desires.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: They who know truth in truth, and untruth in untruth, arrive at truth, and follow true desires.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As rain breaks through an ill-thatched house, passion will break through an unreflecting mind.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As rain does not break through a well-thatched house, passion will not break through a well-reflecting mind.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The evil-doer mourns in this world, and he mourns in the next; he mourns in both. He mourns and suffers when he sees the evil of his own work.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The virtuous man delights in this world, and he delights in the next; he delights in both. He delights and rejoices, when he sees the purity of his own work.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The evil-doer suffers in this world, and he suffers in the next; he suffers in both. He suffers when he thinks of the evil he has done; he suffers more when going on the evil path.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The virtuous man is happy in this world, and he is happy in the next; he is happy in both. He is happy when he thinks of the good he has done; he is still more happy when going on the good path.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The thoughtless man, even if he can recite a large portion (of the law), but is not a doer of it, has no share in the priesthood, but is like a cowherd counting the cows of others.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The follower of the law, even if he can recite only a small portion (of the law), but, having forsaken passion and hatred and foolishness, possesses true knowledge and serenity of mind, he, caring for nothing in this world or that to come, has indeed a share in the priesthood. Chapter II. On Earnestness

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Earnestness is the path of immortality (Nirvana), thoughtlessness the path of death. Those who are in earnest do not die, those who are thoughtless are as if dead already.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Those who are advanced in earnestness, having understood this clearly, delight in earnestness, and rejoice in the knowledge of the Ariyas (the elect).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: These wise people, meditative, steady, always possessed of strong powers, attain to Nirvana, the highest happiness.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If an earnest person has roused himself, if he is not forgetful, if his deeds are pure, if he acts with consideration, if he restrains himself, and lives according to law,--then his glory will increase.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: By rousing himself, by earnestness, by restraint and control, the wise man may make for himself an island which no flood can overwhelm.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Fools follow after vanity, men of evil wisdom. The wise man keeps earnestness as his best jewel.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Follow not after vanity, nor after the enjoyment of love and lust! He who is earnest and meditative, obtains ample joy.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: When the learned man drives away vanity by earnestness, he, the wise, climbing the terraced heights of wisdom, looks down upon the fools, serene he looks upon the toiling crowd, as one that stands on a mountain looks down upon them that stand upon the plain.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Earnest among the thoughtless, awake among the sleepers, the wise man advances like a racer, leaving behind the hack.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: By earnestness did Maghavan (Indra) rise to the lordship of the gods. People praise earnestness; thoughtlessness is always blamed.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in earnestness, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, moves about like fire, burning all his fetters, small or large.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in reflection, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, cannot fall away (from his perfect state)--he is close upon Nirvana. Chapter III. Thought

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As a fletcher makes straight his arrow, a wise man makes straight his trembling and unsteady thought, which is difficult to guard, difficult to hold back.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As a fish taken from his watery home and thrown on dry ground, our thought trembles all over in order to escape the dominion of Mara (the tempter).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: It is good to tame the mind, which is difficult to hold in and flighty, rushing wherever it listeth; a tamed mind brings happiness.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let the wise man guard his thoughts, for they are difficult to perceive, very artful, and they rush wherever they list: thoughts well guarded bring happiness.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Those who bridle their mind which travels far, moves about alone, is without a body, and hides in the chamber (of the heart), will be free from the bonds of Mara (the tempter).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man's thoughts are unsteady, if he does not know the true law, if his peace of mind is troubled, his knowledge will never be perfect.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man's thoughts are not dissipated, if his mind is not perplexed, if he has ceased to think of good or evil, then there is no fear for him while he is watchful.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Knowing that this body is (fragile) like a jar, and making this thought firm like a fortress, one should attack Mara (the tempter) with the weapon of knowledge, one should watch him when conquered, and should never rest.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Before long, alas! this body will lie on the earth, despised, without understanding, like a useless log.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Whatever a hater may do to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a wrongly-directed mind will do us greater mischief.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not a mother, not a father will do so much, nor any other relative; a well-directed mind will do us greater service. Chapter IV. Flowers

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Who shall overcome this earth, and the world of Yama (the lord of the departed), and the world of the gods? Who shall find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower?

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The disciple will overcome the earth, and the world of Yama, and the world of the gods. The disciple will find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who knows that this body is like froth, and has learnt that it is as unsubstantial as a mirage, will break the flower-pointed arrow of Mara, and never see the king of death.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Death carries off a man who is gathering flowers and whose mind is distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Death subdues a man who is gathering flowers, and whose mind is distracted, before he is satiated in his pleasures.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As the bee collects nectar and departs without injuring the flower, or its colour or scent, so let a sage dwell in his village.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not the perversities of others, not their sins of commission or omission, but his own misdeeds and negligences should a sage take notice of.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Like a beautiful flower, full of colour, but without scent, are the fine but fruitless words of him who does not act accordingly.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But, like a beautiful flower, full of colour and full of scent, are the fine and fruitful words of him who acts accordingly.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As many kinds of wreaths can be made from a heap of flowers, so many good things may be achieved by a mortal when once he is born.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor (that of) sandal-wood, or of Tagara and Mallika flowers; but the odour of good people travels even against the wind; a good man pervades every place.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Sandal-wood or Tagara, a lotus-flower, or a Vassiki, among these sorts of perfumes, the perfume of virtue is unsurpassed.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Mean is the scent that comes from Tagara and sandal-wood;--the perfume of those who possess virtue rises up to the gods as the highest.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Of the people who possess these virtues, who live without thoughtlessness, and who are emancipated through true knowledge, Mara, the tempter, never finds the way.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As on a heap of rubbish cast upon the highway the lily will grow full of sweet perfume and delight, thus the disciple of the truly enlightened Buddha shines forth by his knowledge among those who are like rubbish, among the people that walk in darkness. Chapter V. The Fool

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As on a heap of rubbish cast upon the highway the lily will grow full of sweet perfume and delight, thus the disciple of the truly enlightened Buddha shines forth by his knowledge among those who are like rubbish, among the people that walk in darkness. Chapter V. The Fool

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Long is the night to him who is awake; long is a mile to him who is tired; long is life to the foolish who do not know the true law.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a traveller does not meet with one who is his better, or his equal, let him firmly keep to his solitary journey; there is no companionship with a fool.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: "These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me," with such thoughts a fool is tormented. He himself does not belong to himself; how much less sons and wealth?

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The fool who knows his foolishness, is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise, he is called a fool indeed.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a fool be associated with a wise man even all his life, he will perceive the truth as little as a spoon perceives the taste of soup.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If an intelligent man be associated for one minute only with a wise man, he will soon perceive the truth, as the tongue perceives the taste of soup.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Fools of little understanding have themselves for their greatest enemies, for they do evil deeds which must bear bitter fruits.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: That deed is not well done of which a man must repent, and the reward of which he receives crying and with a tearful face.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: No, that deed is well done of which a man does not repent, and the reward of which he receives gladly and cheerfully.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As long as the evil deed done does not bear fruit, the fool thinks it is like honey; but when it ripens, then the fool suffers grief.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let a fool month after month eat his food (like an ascetic) with the tip of a blade of Kusa grass, yet he is not worth the sixteenth particle of those who have well weighed the law.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: An evil deed, like newly-drawn milk, does not turn (suddenly); smouldering, like fire covered by ashes, it follows the fool.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: And when the evil deed, after it has become known, brings sorrow to the fool, then it destroys his bright lot, nay, it cleaves his head.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let the fool wish for a false reputation, for precedence among the Bhikshus, for lordship in the convents, for worship among other people!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: "May both the layman and he who has left the world think that this is done by me; may they be subject to me in everything which is to be done or is not to be done," thus is the mind of the fool, and his desire and pride increase.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: "One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that leads to Nirvana;" if the Bhikshu, the disciple of Buddha, has learnt this, he will not yearn for honour, he will strive after separation from the world. Chapter VI. The Wise Man (Pandita)

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If you see an intelligent man who tells you where true treasures are to be found, who shows what is to be avoided, and administers reproofs, follow that wise man; it will be better, not worse, for those who follow him.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let him admonish, let him teach, let him forbid what is improper!--he will be beloved of the good, by the bad he will be hated.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Do not have evil-doers for friends, do not have low people for friends: have virtuous people for friends, have for friends the best of men.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who drinks in the law lives happily with a serene mind: the sage rejoices always in the law, as preached by the elect (Ariyas).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; wise people fashion themselves.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, wise people falter not amidst blame and praise.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Wise people, after they have listened to the laws, become serene, like a deep, smooth, and still lake.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Good people walk on whatever befall, the good do not prattle, longing for pleasure; whether touched by happiness or sorrow wise people never appear elated or depressed.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If, whether for his own sake, or for the sake of others, a man wishes neither for a son, nor for wealth, nor for lordship, and if he does not wish for his own success by unfair means, then he is good, wise, and virtuous.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Few are there among men who arrive at the other shore (become Arhats); the other people here run up and down the shore.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But those who, when the law has been well preached to them, follow the law, will pass across the dominion of death, however difficult to overcome.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A wise man should leave the dark state (of ordinary life), and follow the bright state (of the Bhikshu). After going from his home to a homeless state, he should in his retirement look for enjoyment where there seemed to be no enjoyment. Leaving all pleasures behind, and calling nothing his own, the wise man should purge himself from all the troubles of the mind.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A wise man should leave the dark state (of ordinary life), and follow the bright state (of the Bhikshu). After going from his home to a homeless state, he should in his retirement look for enjoyment where there seemed to be no enjoyment. Leaving all pleasures behind, and calling nothing his own, the wise man should purge himself from all the troubles of the mind.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Those whose mind is well grounded in the (seven) elements of knowledge, who without clinging to anything, rejoice in freedom from attachment, whose appetites have been conquered, and who are full of light, are free (even) in this world. Chapter VII. The Venerable (Arhat).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: There is no suffering for him who has finished his journey, and abandoned grief, who has freed himself on all sides, and thrown off all fetters.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: They depart with their thoughts well-collected, they are not happy in their abode; like swans who have left their lake, they leave their house and home.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Men who have no riches, who live on recognised food, who have perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), their path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He whose appetites are stilled, who is not absorbed in enjoyment, who has perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), his path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The gods even envy him whose senses, like horses well broken in by the driver, have been subdued, who is free from pride, and free from appetites.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Such a one who does his duty is tolerant like the earth, like Indra's bolt; he is like a lake without mud; no new births are in store for him.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: His thought is quiet, quiet are his word and deed, when he has obtained freedom by true knowledge, when he has thus become a quiet man.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The man who is free from credulity, but knows the uncreated, who has cut all ties, removed all temptations, renounced all desires, he is the greatest of men.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: In a hamlet or in a forest, in the deep water or on the dry land, wherever venerable persons (Arhanta) dwell, that place is delightful.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Forests are delightful; where the world finds no delight, there the passionless will find delight, for they look not for pleasures. Chapter VIII. The Thousands

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Even though a speech be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of sense is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Even though a Gatha (poem) be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of a Gatha is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Though a man recite a hundred Gathas made up of senseless words, one word of the law is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, not Mara with Brahman could change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself, and always lives under restraint.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, not Mara with Brahman could change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself, and always lives under restraint.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man for a hundred years sacrifice month after month with a thousand, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man for a hundred years worship Agni (fire) in the forest, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Whatever a man sacrifice in this world as an offering or as an oblation for a whole year in order to gain merit, the whole of it is not worth a quarter (a farthing); reverence shown to the righteous is better.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who always greets and constantly reveres the aged, four things will increase to him, viz. life, beauty, happiness, power.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But he who lives a hundred years, vicious and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is virtuous and reflecting.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: And he who lives a hundred years, ignorant and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is wise and reflecting.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: And he who lives a hundred years, idle and weak, a life of one day is better if a man has attained firm strength.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing beginning and end, a life of one day is better if a man sees beginning and end.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the immortal place, a life of one day is better if a man sees the immortal place.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the highest law, a life of one day is better if a man sees the highest law. Chapter IX. Evil

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man would hasten towards the good, he should keep his thought away from evil; if a man does what is good slothfully, his mind delights in evil.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man commits a sin, let him not do it again; let him not delight in sin: pain is the outcome of evil.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man does what is good, let him do it again; let him delight in it: happiness is the outcome of good.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Even an evil-doer sees happiness as long as his evil deed has not ripened; but when his evil deed has ripened, then does the evil-doer see evil.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Even a good man sees evil days, as long as his good deed has not ripened; but when his good deed has ripened, then does the good man see happy days.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let no man think lightly of evil, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the fool becomes full of evil, even if he gather it little by little.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let no man think lightly of good, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the wise man becomes full of good, even if he gather it little by little.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let a man avoid evil deeds, as a merchant, if he has few companions and carries much wealth, avoids a dangerous road; as a man who loves life avoids poison.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who has no wound on his hand, may touch poison with his hand; poison does not affect one who has no wound; nor is there evil for one who does not commit evil.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man offend a harmless, pure, and innocent person, the evil falls back upon that fool, like light dust thrown up against the wind.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Some people are born again; evil-doers go to hell; righteous people go to heaven; those who are free from all worldly desires attain Nirvana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where a man might be freed from an evil deed.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where death could not overcome (the mortal). Chapter X. Punishment

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: All men tremble at punishment, all men fear death; remember that you are like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: All men tremble at punishment, all men love life; remember that thou art like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who seeking his own happiness punishes or kills beings who also long for happiness, will not find happiness after death.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who seeking his own happiness does not punish or kill beings who also long for happiness, will find happiness after death.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Do not speak harshly to anybody; those who are spoken to will answer thee in the same way. Angry speech is painful, blows for blows will touch thee.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If, like a shattered metal plate (gong), thou utter not, then thou hast reached Nirvana; contention is not known to thee.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As a cowherd with his staff drives his cows into the stable, so do Age and Death drive the life of men.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A fool does not know when he commits his evil deeds: but the wicked man burns by his own deeds, as if burnt by fire.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who inflicts pain on innocent and harmless persons, will soon come to one of these ten states:

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He will have cruel suffering, loss, injury of the body, heavy affliction, or loss of mind,

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Or a misfortune coming from the king, or a fearful accusation, or loss of relations, or destruction of treasures,

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Or lightning-fire will burn his houses; and when his body is destroyed, the fool will go to hell.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not nakedness, not platted hair, not dirt, not fasting, or lying on the earth, not rubbing with dust, not sitting motionless, can purify a mortal who has not overcome desires.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who, though dressed in fine apparel, exercises tranquillity, is quiet, subdued, restrained, chaste, and has ceased to find fault with all other beings, he indeed is a Brahmana, an ascetic (sramana), a friar (bhikshu).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Is there in this world any man so restrained by humility that he does not mind reproof, as a well-trained horse the whip?

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Like a well-trained horse when touched by the whip, be ye active and lively, and by faith, by virtue, by energy, by meditation, by discernment of the law you will overcome this great pain (of reproof), perfect in knowledge and in behaviour, and never forgetful.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; good people fashion themselves. Chapter XI. Old Age

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: How is there laughter, how is there joy, as this world is always burning? Why do you not seek a light, ye who are surrounded by darkness?

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Look at this dressed-up lump, covered with wounds, joined together, sickly, full of many thoughts, which has no strength, no hold!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: This body is wasted, full of sickness, and frail; this heap of corruption breaks to pieces, life indeed ends in death.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Those white bones, like gourds thrown away in the autumn, what pleasure is there in looking at them?

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: After a stronghold has been made of the bones, it is covered with flesh and blood, and there dwell in it old age and death, pride and deceit.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The brilliant chariots of kings are destroyed, the body also approaches destruction, but the virtue of good people never approaches destruction,--thus do the good say to the good.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man who has learnt little, grows old like an ox; his flesh grows, but his knowledge does not grow.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Looking for the maker of this tabernacle, I shall have to run through a course of many births, so long as I do not find (him); and painful is birth again and again. But now, maker of the tabernacle, thou hast been seen; thou shalt not make up this tabernacle again. All thy rafters are broken, thy ridge-pole is sundered; the mind, approaching the Eternal (visankhara, nirvana), has attained to the extinction of all desires.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Looking for the maker of this tabernacle, I shall have to run through a course of many births, so long as I do not find (him); and painful is birth again and again. But now, maker of the tabernacle, thou hast been seen; thou shalt not make up this tabernacle again. All thy rafters are broken, thy ridge-pole is sundered; the mind, approaching the Eternal (visankhara, nirvana), has attained to the extinction of all desires.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, perish like old herons in a lake without fish.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, lie, like broken bows, sighing after the past. Chapter XII. Self

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man hold himself dear, let him watch himself carefully; during one at least out of the three watches a wise man should be watchful.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let each man direct himself first to what is proper, then let him teach others; thus a wise man will not suffer.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man make himself as he teaches others to be, then, being himself well subdued, he may subdue (others); one's own self is indeed difficult to subdue.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Self is the lord of self, who else could be the lord? With self well subdued, a man finds a lord such as few can find.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The evil done by oneself, self-begotten, self-bred, crushes the foolish, as a diamond breaks a precious stone.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He whose wickedness is very great brings himself down to that state where his enemy wishes him to be, as a creeper does with the tree which it surrounds.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Bad deeds, and deeds hurtful to ourselves, are easy to do; what is beneficial and good, that is very difficult to do.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The foolish man who scorns the rule of the venerable (Arahat), of the elect (Ariya), of the virtuous, and follows false doctrine, he bears fruit to his own destruction, like the fruits of the Katthaka reed.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: By oneself the evil is done, by oneself one suffers; by oneself evil is left undone, by oneself one is purified. Purity and impurity belong to oneself, no one can purify another.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let no one forget his own duty for the sake of another's, however great; let a man, after he has discerned his own duty, be always attentive to his duty. Chapter XIII. The World

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Do not follow the evil law! Do not live on in thoughtlessness! Do not follow false doctrine! Be not a friend of the world.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Rouse thyself! do not be idle! Follow the law of virtue! The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Follow the law of virtue; do not follow that of sin. The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Look upon the world as a bubble, look upon it as a mirage: the king of death does not see him who thus looks down upon the world.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Come, look at this glittering world, like unto a royal chariot; the foolish are immersed in it, but the wise do not touch it.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who formerly was reckless and afterwards became sober, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He whose evil deeds are covered by good deeds, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: This world is dark, few only can see here; a few only go to heaven, like birds escaped from the net.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The swans go on the path of the sun, they go through the ether by means of their miraculous power; the wise are led out of this world, when they have conquered Mara and his train.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man has transgressed one law, and speaks lies, and scoffs at another world, there is no evil he will not do.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The uncharitable do not go to the world of the gods; fools only do not praise liberality; a wise man rejoices in liberality, and through it becomes blessed in the other world.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Better than sovereignty over the earth, better than going to heaven, better than lordship over all worlds, is the reward of the first step in holiness. Chapter XIV. The Buddha (The Awakened)

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He whose conquest is not conquered again, into whose conquest no one in this world enters, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He whom no desire with its snares and poisons can lead astray, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Even the gods envy those who are awakened and not forgetful, who are given to meditation, who are wise, and who delight in the repose of retirement (from the world).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Difficult (to obtain) is the conception of men, difficult is the life of mortals, difficult is the hearing of the True Law, difficult is the birth of the Awakened (the attainment of Buddhahood).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one's mind, that is the teaching of (all) the Awakened.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The Awakened call patience the highest penance, long-suffering the highest Nirvana; for he is not an anchorite (pravragita) who strikes others, he is not an ascetic (sramana) who insults others.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not to blame, not to strike, to live restrained under the law, to be moderate in eating, to sleep and sit alone, and to dwell on the highest thoughts,--this is the teaching of the Awakened.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: There is no satisfying lusts, even by a shower of gold pieces; he who knows that lusts have a short taste and cause pain, he is wise;

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Even in heavenly pleasures he finds no satisfaction, the disciple who is fully awakened delights only in the destruction of all desires.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Men, driven by fear, go to many a refuge, to mountains and forests, to groves and sacred trees.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But that is not a safe refuge, that is not the best refuge; a man is not delivered from all pains after having gone to that refuge.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who takes refuge with Buddha, the Law, and the Church; he who, with clear understanding, sees the four holy truths:--

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Viz. pain, the origin of pain, the destruction of pain, and the eightfold holy way that leads to the quieting of pain;--

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: That is the safe refuge, that is the best refuge; having gone to that refuge, a man is delivered from all pain.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A supernatural person (a Buddha) is not easily found, he is not born everywhere. Wherever such a sage is born, that race prospers.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Happy is the arising of the awakened, happy is the teaching of the True Law, happy is peace in the church, happy is the devotion of those who are at peace.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who pays homage to those who deserve homage, whether the awakened (Buddha) or their disciples, those who have overcome the host (of evils), and crossed the flood of sorrow, he who pays homage to such as have found deliverance and know no fear, his merit can never be measured by anybody. Chapter XV. Happiness

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who pays homage to those who deserve homage, whether the awakened (Buddha) or their disciples, those who have overcome the host (of evils), and crossed the flood of sorrow, he who pays homage to such as have found deliverance and know no fear, his merit can never be measured by anybody. Chapter XV. Happiness

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let us live happily then, not hating those who hate us! among men who hate us let us dwell free from hatred!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let us live happily then, free from ailments among the ailing! among men who are ailing let us dwell free from ailments!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let us live happily then, free from greed among the greedy! among men who are greedy let us dwell free from greed!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let us live happily then, though we call nothing our own! We shall be like the bright gods, feeding on happiness!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Victory breeds hatred, for the conquered is unhappy. He who has given up both victory and defeat, he, the contented, is happy.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: There is no fire like passion; there is no losing throw like hatred; there is no pain like this body; there is no happiness higher than rest.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Hunger is the worst of diseases, the body the greatest of pains; if one knows this truly, that is Nirvana, the highest happiness.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Health is the greatest of gifts, contentedness the best riches; trust is the best of relationships, Nirvana the highest happiness.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who has tasted the sweetness of solitude and tranquillity, is free from fear and free from sin, while he tastes the sweetness of drinking in the law.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The sight of the elect (Arya) is good, to live with them is always happiness; if a man does not see fools, he will be truly happy.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who walks in the company of fools suffers a long way; company with fools, as with an enemy, is always painful; company with the wise is pleasure, like meeting with kinsfolk.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Therefore, one ought to follow the wise, the intelligent, the learned, the much enduring, the dutiful, the elect; one ought to follow a good and wise man, as the moon follows the path of the stars. Chapter XVI. Pleasure

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who gives himself to vanity, and does not give himself to meditation, forgetting the real aim (of life) and grasping at pleasure, will in time envy him who has exerted himself in meditation.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let no man ever look for what is pleasant, or what is unpleasant. Not to see what is pleasant is pain, and it is pain to see what is unpleasant.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let, therefore, no man love anything; loss of the beloved is evil. Those who love nothing and hate nothing, have no fetters.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: From pleasure comes grief, from pleasure comes fear; he who is free from pleasure knows neither grief nor fear.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: From affection comes grief, from affection comes fear; he who is free from affection knows neither grief nor fear.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: From lust comes grief, from lust comes fear; he who is free from lust knows neither grief nor fear.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: From love comes grief, from love comes fear; he who is free from love knows neither grief nor fear.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: From greed comes grief, from greed comes fear; he who is free from greed knows neither grief nor fear.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who possesses virtue and intelligence, who is just, speaks the truth, and does what is his own business, him the world will hold dear.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He in whom a desire for the Ineffable (Nirvana) has sprung up, who is satisfied in his mind, and whose thoughts are not bewildered by love, he is called urdhvamsrotas (carried upwards by the stream).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Kinsmen, friends, and lovers salute a man who has been long away, and returns safe from afar.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: In like manner his good works receive him who has done good, and has gone from this world to the other;--as kinsmen receive a friend on his return. Chapter XVII. Anger

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let a man leave anger, let him forsake pride, let him overcome all bondage! No sufferings befall the man who is not attached to name and form, and who calls nothing his own.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who holds back rising anger like a rolling chariot, him I call a real driver; other people are but holding the reins.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good; let him overcome the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Speak the truth, do not yield to anger; give, if thou art asked for little; by these three steps thou wilt go near the gods.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The sages who injure nobody, and who always control their body, they will go to the unchangeable place (Nirvana), where, if they have gone, they will suffer no more.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Those who are ever watchful, who study day and night, and who strive after Nirvana, their passions will come to an end.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: This is an old saying, O Atula, this is not only of to-day: `They blame him who sits silent, they blame him who speaks much, they also blame him who says little; there is no one on earth who is not blamed.'

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: There never was, there never will be, nor is there now, a man who is always blamed, or a man who is always praised.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But he whom those who discriminate praise continually day after day, as without blemish, wise, rich in knowledge and virtue, who would dare to blame him, like a coin made of gold from the Gambu river? Even the gods praise him, he is praised even by Brahman.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But he whom those who discriminate praise continually day after day, as without blemish, wise, rich in knowledge and virtue, who would dare to blame him, like a coin made of gold from the Gambu river? Even the gods praise him, he is praised even by Brahman.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Beware of bodily anger, and control thy body! Leave the sins of the body, and with thy body practise virtue!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Beware of the anger of the tongue, and control thy tongue! Leave the sins of the tongue, and practise virtue with thy tongue!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Beware of the anger of the mind, and control thy mind! Leave the sins of the mind, and practise virtue with thy mind!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The wise who control their body, who control their tongue, the wise who control their mind, are indeed well controlled. Chapter XVIII. Impurity

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Thou art now like a sear leaf, the messengers of death (Yama) have come near to thee; thou standest at the door of thy departure, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt enter into the heavenly world of the elect (Ariya).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Thy life has come to an end, thou art come near to death (Yama), there is no resting-place for thee on the road, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.
