metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt not enter again into birth and decay.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let a wise man blow off the impurities of his self, as a smith blows off the impurities of silver one by one, little by little, and from time to time.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As the impurity which springs from the iron, when it springs from it, destroys it; thus do a transgressor's own works lead him to the evil path.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The taint of prayers is non-repetition; the taint of houses, non-repair; the taint of the body is sloth; the taint of a watchman, thoughtlessness.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Bad conduct is the taint of woman, greediness the taint of a benefactor; tainted are all evil ways in this world and in the next.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But there is a taint worse than all taints,--ignorance is the greatest taint. O mendicants! throw off that taint, and become taintless!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Life is easy to live for a man who is without shame, a crow hero, a mischief-maker, an insulting, bold, and wretched fellow.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But life is hard to live for a modest man, who always looks for what is pure, who is disinterested, quiet, spotless, and intelligent.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who destroys life, who speaks untruth, who in this world takes what is not given him, who goes to another man's wife;

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: And the man who gives himself to drinking intoxicating liquors, he, even in this world, digs up his own root.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: O man, know this, that the unrestrained are in a bad state; take care that greediness and vice do not bring thee to grief for a long time!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The world gives according to their faith or according to their pleasure: if a man frets about the food and the drink given to others, he will find no rest either by day or by night.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He in whom that feeling is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, finds rest by day and by night.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: There is no fire like passion, there is no shark like hatred, there is no snare like folly, there is no torrent like greed.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The fault of others is easily perceived, but that of oneself is difficult to perceive; a man winnows his neighbour's faults like chaff, but his own fault he hides, as a cheat hides the bad die from the gambler.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man looks after the faults of others, and is always inclined to be offended, his own passions will grow, and he is far from the destruction of passions.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. The world delights in vanity, the Tathagatas (the Buddhas) are free from vanity.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. No creatures are eternal; but the awakened (Buddha) are never shaken. Chapter XIX. The Just

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not just if he carries a matter by violence; no, he who distinguishes both right and wrong, who is learned and leads others, not by violence, but by law and equity, and who is guarded by the law and intelligent, he is called just.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not just if he carries a matter by violence; no, he who distinguishes both right and wrong, who is learned and leads others, not by violence, but by law and equity, and who is guarded by the law and intelligent, he is called just.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not learned because he talks much; he who is patient, free from hatred and fear, he is called learned.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not a supporter of the law because he talks much; even if a man has learnt little, but sees the law bodily, he is a supporter of the law, a man who never neglects the law.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not an elder because his head is grey; his age may be ripe, but he is called `Old-in-vain.'

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He in whom there is truth, virtue, love, restraint, moderation, he who is free from impurity and is wise, he is called an elder.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: An envious greedy, dishonest man does not become respectable by means of much talking only, or by the beauty of his complexion.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He in whom all this is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, he, when freed from hatred and wise, is called respectable.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not by tonsure does an undisciplined man who speaks falsehood become a Samana; can a man be a Samana who is still held captive by desire and greediness?

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who always quiets the evil, whether small or large, he is called a Samana (a quiet man), because he has quieted all evil.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not a mendicant (Bhikshu) simply because he asks others for alms; he who adopts the whole law is a Bhikshu, not he who only begs.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who is above good and evil, who is chaste, who with knowledge passes through the world, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not a Muni because he observes silence (mona, i.e. mauna), if he is foolish and ignorant; but the wise who, taking the balance, chooses the good and avoids evil, he is a Muni, and is a Muni thereby; he who in this world weighs both sides is called a Muni.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not a Muni because he observes silence (mona, i.e. mauna), if he is foolish and ignorant; but the wise who, taking the balance, chooses the good and avoids evil, he is a Muni, and is a Muni thereby; he who in this world weighs both sides is called a Muni.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man is not an elect (Ariya) because he injures living creatures; because he has pity on all living creatures, therefore is a man called Ariya.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not only by discipline and vows, not only by much learning, not by entering into a trance, not by sleeping alone, do I earn the happiness of release which no worldling can know. Bhikshu, be not confident as long as thou hast not attained the extinction of desires. Chapter XX. The Way

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Not only by discipline and vows, not only by much learning, not by entering into a trance, not by sleeping alone, do I earn the happiness of release which no worldling can know. Bhikshu, be not confident as long as thou hast not attained the extinction of desires. Chapter XX. The Way

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The best of ways is the eightfold; the best of truths the four words; the best of virtues passionlessness; the best of men he who has eyes to see.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: This is the way, there is no other that leads to the purifying of intelligence. Go on this way! Everything else is the deceit of Mara (the tempter).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If you go on this way, you will make an end of pain! The way was preached by me, when I had understood the removal of the thorns (in the flesh).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: You yourself must make an effort. The Tathagatas (Buddhas) are only preachers. The thoughtful who enter the way are freed from the bondage of Mara.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: `All created things perish,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way to purity.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: `All created things are grief and pain,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: `All forms are unreal,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who does not rouse himself when it is time to rise, who, though young and strong, is full of sloth, whose will and thought are weak, that lazy and idle man will never find the way to knowledge.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Watching his speech, well restrained in mind, let a man never commit any wrong with his body! Let a man but keep these three roads of action clear, and he will achieve the way which is taught by the wise.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Through zeal knowledge is gotten, through lack of zeal knowledge is lost; let a man who knows this double path of gain and loss thus place himself that knowledge may grow.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Cut down the whole forest (of lust), not a tree only! Danger comes out of the forest (of lust). When you have cut down both the forest (of lust) and its undergrowth, then, Bhikshus, you will be rid of the forest and free!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: So long as the love of man towards women, even the smallest, is not destroyed, so long is his mind in bondage, as the calf that drinks milk is to its mother.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Cut out the love of self, like an autumn lotus, with thy hand! Cherish the road of peace. Nirvana has been shown by Sugata (Buddha).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: `Here I shall dwell in the rain, here in winter and summer,' thus the fool meditates, and does not think of his death.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Death comes and carries off that man, praised for his children and flocks, his mind distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Sons are no help, nor a father, nor relations; there is no help from kinsfolk for one whom death has seized.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A wise and good man who knows the meaning of this, should quickly clear the way that leads to Nirvana. Chapter XXI. Miscellaneous

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a wise man leave the small pleasure, and look to the great.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who, by causing pain to others, wishes to obtain pleasure for himself, he, entangled in the bonds of hatred, will never be free from hatred.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: What ought to be done is neglected, what ought not to be done is done; the desires of unruly, thoughtless people are always increasing.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: But they whose whole watchfulness is always directed to their body, who do not follow what ought not to be done, and who steadfastly do what ought to be done, the desires of such watchful and wise people will come to an end.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two valiant kings, though he has destroyed a kingdom with all its subjects.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two holy kings, and an eminent man besides.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The disciples of Gotama (Buddha) are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on Buddha.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the law.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the church.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on their body.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in compassion.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in meditation.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: It is hard to leave the world (to become a friar), it is hard to enjoy the world; hard is the monastery, painful are the houses; painful it is to dwell with equals (to share everything in common) and the itinerant mendicant is beset with pain. Therefore let no man be an itinerant mendicant and he will not be beset with pain.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Whatever place a faithful, virtuous, celebrated, and wealthy man chooses, there he is respected.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Good people shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; bad people are not seen, like arrows shot by night.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He alone who, without ceasing, practises the duty of sitting alone and sleeping alone, he, subduing himself, will rejoice in the destruction of all desires alone, as if living in a forest. Chapter XXII. The Downward Course

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who says what is not, goes to hell; he also who, having done a thing, says I have not done it. After death both are equal, they are men with evil deeds in the next world.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Many men whose shoulders are covered with the yellow gown are ill-conditioned and unrestrained; such evil-doers by their evil deeds go to hell.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Better it would be to swallow a heated iron ball, like flaring fire, than that a bad unrestrained fellow should live on the charity of the land.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's wife,--a bad reputation, an uncomfortable bed, thirdly, punishment, and lastly, hell.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is the short pleasure of the frightened in the arms of the frightened, and the king imposes heavy punishment; therefore let no man think of his neighbour's wife.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm, badly-practised asceticism leads to hell.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and hesitating obedience to discipline, all this brings no great reward.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it vigorously! A careless pilgrim only scatters the dust of his passions more widely.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it afterwards; a good deed is better done, for having done it, one does not repent.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and without, so let a man guard himself. Not a moment should escape, for they who allow the right moment to pass, suffer pain when they are in hell.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of, and are not ashamed of what they ought to be ashamed of, such men, embracing false doctrines enter the evil path.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when they ought to fear, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and forbid not when there is something to be forbidden, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not forbidden as not forbidden, such men, embracing the true doctrine, enter the good path. Chapter XXIII. The Elephant

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Silently shall I endure abuse as the elephant in battle endures the arrow sent from the bow: for the world is ill-natured.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: They lead a tamed elephant to battle, the king mounts a tamed elephant; the tamed is the best among men, he who silently endures abuse.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Mules are good, if tamed, and noble Sindhu horses, and elephants with large tusks; but he who tames himself is better still.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: For with these animals does no man reach the untrodden country (Nirvana), where a tamed man goes on a tamed animal, viz. on his own well-tamed self.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The elephant called Dhanapalaka, his temples running with sap, and difficult to hold, does not eat a morsel when bound; the elephant longs for the elephant grove.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man becomes fat and a great eater, if he is sleepy and rolls himself about, that fool, like a hog fed on wash, is born again and again.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: This mind of mine went formerly wandering about as it liked, as it listed, as it pleased; but I shall now hold it in thoroughly, as the rider who holds the hook holds in the furious elephant.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Be not thoughtless, watch your thoughts! Draw yourself out of the evil way, like an elephant sunk in mud.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man find a prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, he may walk with him, overcoming all dangers, happy, but considerate.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man find no prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, let him walk alone, like a king who has left his conquered country behind,--like an elephant in the forest.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: It is better to live alone, there is no companionship with a fool; let a man walk alone, let him commit no sin, with few wishes, like an elephant in the forest.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If an occasion arises, friends are pleasant; enjoyment is pleasant, whatever be the cause; a good work is pleasant in the hour of death; the giving up of all grief is pleasant.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Pleasant in the world is the state of a mother, pleasant the state of a father, pleasant the state of a Samana, pleasant the state of a Brahmana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Pleasant is virtue lasting to old age, pleasant is a faith firmly rooted; pleasant is attainment of intelligence, pleasant is avoiding of sins. Chapter XXIV. Thirst

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The thirst of a thoughtless man grows like a creeper; he runs from life to life, like a monkey seeking fruit in the forest.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Whomsoever this fierce thirst overcomes, full of poison, in this world, his sufferings increase like the abounding Birana grass.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who overcomes this fierce thirst, difficult to be conquered in this world, sufferings fall off from him, like water-drops from a lotus leaf.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: This salutary word I tell you, `Do ye, as many as are here assembled, dig up the root of thirst, as he who wants the sweet-scented Usira root must dig up the Birana grass, that Mara (the tempter) may not crush you again and again, as the stream crushes the reeds.'

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As a tree, even though it has been cut down, is firm so long as its root is safe, and grows again, thus, unless the feeders of thirst are destroyed, the pain (of life) will return again and again.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He whose thirst running towards pleasure is exceeding strong in the thirty-six channels, the waves will carry away that misguided man, viz. his desires which are set on passion.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The channels run everywhere, the creeper (of passion) stands sprouting; if you see the creeper springing up, cut its root by means of knowledge.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A creature's pleasures are extravagant and luxurious; sunk in lust and looking for pleasure, men undergo (again and again) birth and decay.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; held in fetters and bonds, they undergo pain for a long time, again and again.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; let therefore the mendicant drive out thirst, by striving after passionlessness for himself.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who having got rid of the forest (of lust) (i.e. after having reached Nirvana) gives himself over to forest-life (i.e. to lust), and who, when removed from the forest (i.e. from lust), runs to the forest (i.e. to lust), look at that man! though free, he runs into bondage.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Wise people do not call that a strong fetter which is made of iron, wood, or hemp; far stronger is the care for precious stones and rings, for sons and a wife.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: That fetter wise people call strong which drags down, yields, but is difficult to undo; after having cut this at last, people leave the world, free from cares, and leaving desires and pleasures behind.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Those who are slaves to passions, run down with the stream (of desires), as a spider runs down the web which he has made himself; when they have cut this, at last, wise people leave the world free from cares, leaving all affection behind.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Give up what is before, give up what is behind, give up what is in the middle, when thou goest to the other shore of existence; if thy mind is altogether free, thou wilt not again enter into birth and decay.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man is tossed about by doubts, full of strong passions, and yearning only for what is delightful, his thirst will grow more and more, and he will indeed make his fetters strong.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If a man delights in quieting doubts, and, always reflecting, dwells on what is not delightful (the impurity of the body, &c.), he certainly will remove, nay, he will cut the fetter of Mara.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who has reached the consummation, who does not tremble, who is without thirst and without sin, he has broken all the thorns of life: this will be his last body.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who is without thirst and without affection, who understands the words and their interpretation, who knows the order of letters (those which are before and which are after), he has received his last body, he is called the great sage, the great man.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: `I have conquered all, I know all, in all conditions of life I am free from taint; I have left all, and through the destruction of thirst I am free; having learnt myself, whom shall I teach?'

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The gift of the law exceeds all gifts; the sweetness of the law exceeds all sweetness; the delight in the law exceeds all delights; the extinction of thirst overcomes all pain.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Pleasures destroy the foolish, if they look not for the other shore; the foolish by his thirst for pleasures destroys himself, as if he were his own enemy.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by passion: therefore a gift bestowed on the passionless brings great reward.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by hatred: therefore a gift bestowed on those who do not hate brings great reward.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by vanity: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from vanity brings great reward.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by lust: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from lust brings great reward. Chapter XXV. The Bhikshu (Mendicant)

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Restraint in the eye is good, good is restraint in the ear, in the nose restraint is good, good is restraint in the tongue.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: In the body restraint is good, good is restraint in speech, in thought restraint is good, good is restraint in all things. A Bhikshu, restrained in all things, is freed from all pain.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who controls his hand, he who controls his feet, he who controls his speech, he who is well controlled, he who delights inwardly, who is collected, who is solitary and content, him they call Bhikshu.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The Bhikshu who controls his mouth, who speaks wisely and calmly, who teaches the meaning and the law, his word is sweet.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who dwells in the law, delights in the law, meditates on the law, follows the law, that Bhikshu will never fall away from the true law.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let him not despise what he has received, nor ever envy others: a mendicant who envies others does not obtain peace of mind.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A Bhikshu who, though he receives little, does not despise what he has received, even the gods will praise him, if his life is pure, and if he is not slothful.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who never identifies himself with name and form, and does not grieve over what is no more, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The Bhikshu who acts with kindness, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha, will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: O Bhikshu, empty this boat! if emptied, it will go quickly; having cut off passion and hatred thou wilt go to Nirvana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Cut off the five (senses), leave the five, rise above the five. A Bhikshu, who has escaped from the five fetters, he is called Oghatinna, `saved from the flood.'

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Meditate, O Bhikshu, and be not heedless! Do not direct thy thought to what gives pleasure that thou mayest not for thy heedlessness have to swallow the iron ball (in hell), and that thou mayest not cry out when burning, `This is pain.'

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Without knowledge there is no meditation, without meditation there is no knowledge: he who has knowledge and meditation is near unto Nirvana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A Bhikshu who has entered his empty house, and whose mind is tranquil, feels a more than human delight when he sees the law clearly.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As soon as he has considered the origin and destruction of the elements (khandha) of the body, he finds happiness and joy which belong to those who know the immortal (Nirvana).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: And this is the beginning here for a wise Bhikshu: watchfulness over the senses, contentedness, restraint under the law; keep noble friends whose life is pure, and who are not slothful.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Let him live in charity, let him be perfect in his duties; then in the fulness of delight he will make an end of suffering.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: As the Vassika plant sheds its withered flowers, men should shed passion and hatred, O ye Bhikshus!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The Bhikshu whose body and tongue and mind are quieted, who is collected, and has rejected the baits of the world, he is called quiet.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Rouse thyself by thyself, examine thyself by thyself, thus self-protected and attentive wilt thou live happily, O Bhikshu!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: For self is the lord of self, self is the refuge of self; therefore curb thyself as the merchant curbs a good horse.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The Bhikshu, full of delight, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who, even as a young Bhikshu, applies himself to the doctrine of Buddha, brightens up this world, like the moon when free from clouds. Chapter XXVI. The Brahmana (Arhat)

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Stop the stream valiantly, drive away the desires, O Brahmana! When you have understood the destruction of all that was made, you will understand that which was not made.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: If the Brahmana has reached the other shore in both laws (in restraint and contemplation), all bonds vanish from him who has obtained knowledge.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He for whom there is neither this nor that shore, nor both, him, the fearless and unshackled, I call indeed a Brahmana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: He who is thoughtful, blameless, settled, dutiful, without passions, and who has attained the highest end, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The sun is bright by day, the moon shines by night, the warrior is bright in his armour, the Brahmana is bright in his meditation; but Buddha, the Awakened, is bright with splendour day and night.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Because a man is rid of evil, therefore he is called Brahmana; because he walks quietly, therefore he is called Samana; because he has sent away his own impurities, therefore he is called Pravragita (Pabbagita, a pilgrim).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: No one should attack a Brahmana, but no Brahmana (if attacked) should let himself fly at his aggressor! Woe to him who strikes a Brahmana, more woe to him who flies at his aggressor!

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: It advantages a Brahmana not a little if he holds his mind back from the pleasures of life; when all wish to injure has vanished, pain will cease.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not offend by body, word, or thought, and is controlled on these three points.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: After a man has once understood the law as taught by the Well-awakened (Buddha), let him worship it carefully, as the Brahmana worships the sacrificial fire.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: A man does not become a Brahmana by his platted hair, by his family, or by birth; in whom there is truth and righteousness, he is blessed, he is a Brahmana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: What is the use of platted hair, O fool! what of the raiment of goat-skins? Within thee there is ravening, but the outside thou makest clean.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: The man who wears dirty raiments, who is emaciated and covered with veins, who lives alone in the forest, and meditates, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: I do not call a man a Brahmana because of his origin or of his mother. He is indeed arrogant, and he is wealthy: but the poor, who is free from all attachments, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut all fetters, who never trembles, is independent and unshackled.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut the strap and the thong, the chain with all that pertains to it, who has burst the bar, and is awakened.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, though he has committed no offence, endures reproach, bonds, and stripes, who has endurance for his force, and strength for his army.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is free from anger, dutiful, virtuous, without appetite, who is subdued, and has received his last body.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not cling to pleasures, like water on a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed on the point of a needle.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, even here, knows the end of his suffering, has put down his burden, and is unshackled.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose knowledge is deep, who possesses wisdom, who knows the right way and the wrong, and has attained the highest end.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who keeps aloof both from laymen and from mendicants, who frequents no houses, and has but few desires.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who finds no fault with other beings, whether feeble or strong, and does not kill nor cause slaughter.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is tolerant with the intolerant, mild with fault-finders, and free from passion among the passionate.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana from whom anger and hatred, pride and envy have dropt like a mustard seed from the point of a needle.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who utters true speech, instructive and free from harshness, so that he offend no one.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who takes nothing in the world that is not given him, be it long or short, small or large, good or bad.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who fosters no desires for this world or for the next, has no inclinations, and is unshackled.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has no interests, and when he has understood (the truth), does not say How, how? and who has reached the depth of the Immortal.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world is above good and evil, above the bondage of both, free from grief from sin, and from impurity.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is bright like the moon, pure, serene, undisturbed, and in whom all gaiety is extinct.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has traversed this miry road, the impassable world and its vanity, who has gone through, and reached the other shore, is thoughtful, guileless, free from doubts, free from attachment, and content.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world, leaving all desires, travels about without a home, and in whom all concupiscence is extinct.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, leaving all longings, travels about without a home, and in whom all covetousness is extinct.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, after leaving all bondage to men, has risen above all bondage to the gods, and is free from all and every bondage.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has left what gives pleasure and what gives pain, who is cold, and free from all germs (of renewed life), the hero who has conquered all the worlds.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows the destruction and the return of beings everywhere, who is free from bondage, welfaring (Sugata), and awakened (Buddha).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose path the gods do not know, nor spirits (Gandharvas), nor men, whose passions are extinct, and who is an Arhat (venerable).

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who calls nothing his own, whether it be before, behind, or between, who is poor, and free from the love of the world.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana, the manly, the noble, the hero, the great sage, the conqueror, the impassible, the accomplished, the awakened.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows his former abodes, who sees heaven and hell, has reached the end of births, is perfect in knowledge, a sage, and whose perfections are all perfect.

metadata.edition: Sacred Books of the East vol. X, Oxford, 1881 (Project Gutenberg #2017)

translationText: Long Discourses 31

ru.text: Дигха Никая 31

translationText: Advice to Sigālaka

ru.text: Наставление Сингале

translationText: So I have heard.

ru.text: Так я слышал:

rootText: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
translationText: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.

ru.text: однажды Благословенный жил в Раджагахе, в бамбуковой роще, в месте кормления белок.

rootText: Tena kho pana samayena siṅgālako gahapatiputto kālasseva uṭṭhāya rājagahā nikkhamitvā allavattho allakeso pañjaliko puthudisā namassati—
translationText: Now at that time the householder’s son Sigālaka rose early and left Rājagaha. With his clothes and hair all wet, he raised his cupped palms to revere the various quarters—

ru.text: В то время Сингалака — сын домохозяина, поднявшись рано утром, выйдя из Раджагахи, с влажной одеждой и влажными волосами, поклонялся соединёнными вместе руками различным сторонам света —

rootText: puratthimaṁ disaṁ dakkhiṇaṁ disaṁ pacchimaṁ disaṁ uttaraṁ disaṁ heṭṭhimaṁ disaṁ uparimaṁ disaṁ.
translationText: east, south, west, north, below, and above.

ru.text: востоку, югу, западу, северу, надиру и зениту.

rootText: Atha kho bhagavā pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya rājagahaṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi.
translationText: Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Rājagaha for alms.

ru.text: Тогда Благословенный встал рано утром, оделся и, взяв одеяние и сосуд для подаяния, отправился в Раджагаху за едой.

rootText: Addasā kho bhagavā siṅgālakaṁ gahapatiputtaṁ kālasseva vuṭṭhāya rājagahā nikkhamitvā allavatthaṁ allakesaṁ pañjalikaṁ puthudisā namassantaṁ—
translationText: He saw Sigālaka revering the various quarters

ru.text: И Благословенный увидел Сингалаку — сына домохозяина, вставшего рано утром, вышедшего из Раджагахи с влажной одеждой и влажными волосами, поклоняющегося соединёнными вместе руками различным сторонам света —

rootText: puratthimaṁ disaṁ dakkhiṇaṁ disaṁ pacchimaṁ disaṁ uttaraṁ disaṁ heṭṭhimaṁ disaṁ uparimaṁ disaṁ.

ru.text: востоку, югу, западу, северу, надиру и зениту.

translationText: and said to him,

ru.text: Увидев Сингалаку — сына домохозяина Благословенный сказал ему так:

rootText: “kiṁ nu kho tvaṁ, gahapatiputta, kālasseva vuṭṭhāya rājagahā nikkhamitvā allavattho allakeso pañjaliko puthudisā namassasi—
translationText: “Householder’s son, why are you revering the various quarters in this way?”

ru.text: «Почему вы, о сын домохозяина, встали рано утром, и, выйдя из Раджагахи с влажной одеждой и влажными волосами, поклоняетесь соединёнными вместе руками различным сторонам света —

rootText: puratthimaṁ disaṁ dakkhiṇaṁ disaṁ pacchimaṁ disaṁ uttaraṁ disaṁ heṭṭhimaṁ disaṁ uparimaṁ disan”ti?

ru.text: востоку, югу, западу, северу, надиру и зениту?»

rootText: “Pitā maṁ, bhante, kālaṁ karonto evaṁ avaca:
translationText: “Sir, on his deathbed my father said to me:

ru.text: «О досточтимый, мой отец при смерти сказал мне:

translationText: ‘My dear, please revere the quarters.’

ru.text: „Милый, тебе надлежит поклоняться сторонам света“.

rootText: So kho ahaṁ, bhante, pituvacanaṁ sakkaronto garuṁ karonto mānento pūjento kālasseva uṭṭhāya rājagahā nikkhamitvā allavattho allakeso pañjaliko puthudisā namassāmi—
translationText: Honoring, respecting, and venerating my father’s words, I rose early and left Rājagaha and, with my clothes and hair all wet, raised my cupped palms to revere the various quarters—

ru.text: Я, уважая, чтя, почитая и соблюдая это слово моего отца, встал рано утром, вышел из Раджагахи с влажной одеждой и влажными волосами, и поклоняюсь соединёнными вместе руками различным сторонам света —

rootText: puratthimaṁ disaṁ dakkhiṇaṁ disaṁ pacchimaṁ disaṁ uttaraṁ disaṁ heṭṭhimaṁ disaṁ uparimaṁ disan”ti.
translationText: east, south, west, north, below, and above.”

ru.text: востоку, югу, западу, северу, надиру и зениту.»

translationText: 1. The Six Directions

ru.text: 1. Шесть сторон света

rootText: “Na kho, gahapatiputta, ariyassa vinaye evaṁ cha disā namassitabbā”ti.
translationText: “Householder’s son, that’s not how the six directions should be revered in the training of the Noble One.”

ru.text: «О сын домохозяина, не так нужно поклоняться шести сторонам света согласно дисциплине благородных».

rootText: “Yathā kathaṁ pana, bhante, ariyassa vinaye cha disā namassitabbā?
translationText: “But sir, how should the six directions be revered in the training of the Noble One?

ru.text: «Но как же, о досточтимый, нужно поклоняться шести сторонам света согласно дисциплине благородных?

rootText: Sādhu me, bhante, bhagavā tathā dhammaṁ desetu, yathā ariyassa vinaye cha disā namassitabbā”ti.
translationText: Sir, please teach me this.”

ru.text: Было бы хорошо, о досточтимый, если Благословенный преподал бы мне учение о том, как нужно поклоняться шести сторонам света согласно дисциплине благородных.»

rootText: “Tena hi, gahapatiputta, suṇohi sādhukaṁ manasikarohi bhāsissāmī”ti.
translationText: “Well then, householder’s son, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”

ru.text: «Тогда, о сын домохозяина, слушай и тщательно внимай, я буду говорить.»

rootText: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho siṅgālako gahapatiputto bhagavato paccassosi.
translationText: “Yes, sir,” replied Sigālaka.

ru.text: «Да будет так, досточтимый», — отвечал Благословенному Сингалака, сын домохозяина.

translationText: The Buddha said this:

ru.text: И Благословенный говорил так:

rootText: “Yato kho, gahapatiputta, ariyasāvakassa cattāro kammakilesā pahīnā honti, catūhi ca ṭhānehi pāpakammaṁ na karoti, cha ca bhogānaṁ apāyamukhāni na sevati, so evaṁ cuddasa pāpakāpagato chaddisāpaṭicchādī
translationText: “Householder’s son, a noble disciple gives up four corrupt deeds, doesn’t do bad deeds on four grounds, and avoids six drains on wealth. When they’ve left these fourteen bad things behind they have the six directions covered.

ru.text: «О сын домохозяина, ввиду того, что ученик благородных (1) отказался от четырёх загрязняющих (загрязнённых) поступков, (2) не совершает дурных поступков по четырём основаниям, (3) избегает шести каналов растраты богатства, он таким образом отстранился от этих четырнадцати порочных вещей, с защищёнными шестью сторонами света

rootText: ubholokavijayāya paṭipanno hoti. Tassa ayañceva loko āraddho hoti paro ca loko.
translationText: They’re practicing to win in both worlds, and they succeed in this world and the next.

ru.text: вступает на путь, ведущий к победе в обоих мирах. Он благополучен в этом самом мире и в следующем.

rootText: So kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā sugatiṁ saggaṁ lokaṁ upapajjati.
translationText: When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.

ru.text: После разрушения тела, после смерти, он возрождается в благом уделе, в счастливом мире.

translationText: 2. Four Corrupt Deeds

ru.text: 2. Четыре загрязняющих поступка

translationText: What four corrupt deeds have they given up?

ru.text: „Каковы четыре загрязняющих поступка, от которых он отказался?

rootText: Pāṇātipāto kho, gahapatiputta, kammakileso, adinnādānaṁ kammakileso, kāmesumicchācāro kammakileso, musāvādo kammakileso.
translationText: Killing living creatures, stealing, sexual misconduct, and lying: these are corrupt deeds.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, убийство живых существ — это загрязняющий поступок, взятие неданного — загрязняющий поступок, дурное сексуальное поведение — загрязняющий поступок, ложная речь — загрязняющий поступок.

translationText: These are the four corrupt deeds they’ve given up.”

ru.text: Таковы четыре загрязняющих поступка, от которых он отказался“.

translationText: That is what the Buddha said.

ru.text: Так сказал Благословенный.

rootText: Idaṁ vatvāna sugato athāparaṁ etadavoca satthā:
translationText: Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say:

ru.text: Сказав это, Достигший блага учитель добавил:

translationText: “Killing, stealing,

ru.text: „Убийство живых существ, взятие неданного,

translationText: telling lies,

ru.text: произнесение ложных речей

translationText: and committing adultery:

ru.text: и посещение чужой жены

translationText: astute people don’t praise these things.”

ru.text: мудрые не хвалят“.

translationText: 3. Four Grounds

ru.text: 3. Четыре основания

translationText: “On what four grounds do they not do bad deeds?

ru.text: „По каким четырём основаниям он не совершает дурных поступков?

rootText: Chandāgatiṁ gacchanto pāpakammaṁ karoti, dosāgatiṁ gacchanto pāpakammaṁ karoti, mohāgatiṁ gacchanto pāpakammaṁ karoti, bhayāgatiṁ gacchanto pāpakammaṁ karoti.
translationText: One does bad deeds prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice.

ru.text: Человек совершает дурной поступок, последовав дурному образу поведения под действием желания, совершает дурной поступок, последовав дурному образу поведения под действием отвращения, совершает дурной поступок, последовав дурному образу поведения под действием заблуждения, совершает дурной поступок, последовав дурному образу поведения под действием страха.

rootText: Yato kho, gahapatiputta, ariyasāvako neva chandāgatiṁ gacchati, na dosāgatiṁ gacchati, na mohāgatiṁ gacchati, na bhayāgatiṁ gacchati;
translationText: When a noble disciple is not prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice,

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, поскольку последователь благородных не следует дурному образу поведения под действием желания, не следует дурному образу поведения под действием отвращения, не следует дурному образу поведения под действием заблуждения, не следует дурному образу поведения под действием страха,

rootText: imehi catūhi ṭhānehi pāpakammaṁ na karotī”ti.
translationText: they don’t do bad deeds on these four grounds.”

ru.text: он не совершает дурных поступков по этим четырём основаниям.“

translationText: That is what the Buddha said.

ru.text: Так сказал Благословенный.

rootText: Idaṁ vatvāna sugato athāparaṁ etadavoca satthā:
translationText: Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say:

ru.text: Сказав это, Достигший блага учитель добавил:

translationText: “If you act against the teaching

ru.text: „У того, кто из-за желания, отвращения, заблуждения или страха

translationText: out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, or stupidity,

ru.text: преступает Дхамму,

translationText: your fame shrinks,

ru.text: репутация утрачивается как луна,

translationText: like the moon in the waning fortnight.

ru.text: идущая на убыль.

translationText: If you don’t act against the teaching

ru.text: У того, кто из-за желания, отвращения, заблуждения или страха

translationText: out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, and stupidity,

ru.text: не преступает Дхамму

translationText: your fame swells,

ru.text: репутация увеличивается как

translationText: like the moon in the waxing fortnight.”

ru.text: растущая луна“.

translationText: 4. Six Drains on Wealth

ru.text: 4. Шесть каналов растраты богатства

translationText: “What six drains on wealth do they avoid?

ru.text: „Каковы шесть каналов растраты богатства, которых он избегает?

rootText: Surāmerayamajjappamādaṭṭhānānuyogo kho, gahapatiputta, bhogānaṁ apāyamukhaṁ, vikālavisikhācariyānuyogo bhogānaṁ apāyamukhaṁ, samajjābhicaraṇaṁ bhogānaṁ apāyamukhaṁ, jūtappamādaṭṭhānānuyogo bhogānaṁ apāyamukhaṁ, pāpamittānuyogo bhogānaṁ apāyamukhaṁ, ālasyānuyogo bhogānaṁ apāyamukhaṁ.
translationText: Habitually engaging in the following things is a drain on wealth: intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor; roaming the streets at night; frequenting festivals; gambling; bad friends; laziness.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, употребление опьяняющих веществ, приводящих к беспечности, является каналом растраты богатства, разгуливание по улицам в неподходящее время является каналом растраты богатства, частое посещение ярмарок является каналом растраты богатства, пристрастие к азартным играм, вызывающим беспечность, является каналом растраты богатства, связь с порочными друзьями является каналом растраты богатства, приверженность лени является каналом растраты богатства.

translationText: 5. Six Drawbacks of Drinking

ru.text: 5. Шесть опасностей опьяняющих веществ

translationText: There are these six drawbacks of habitually consuming intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, таковы шесть опасностей употребления опьяняющих веществ, приводящих к беспечности.

rootText: Sandiṭṭhikā dhanajāni, kalahappavaḍḍhanī, rogānaṁ āyatanaṁ, akittisañjananī, kopīnanidaṁsanī, paññāya dubbalikaraṇītveva chaṭṭhaṁ padaṁ bhavati.
translationText: Immediate loss of wealth, promotion of quarrels, susceptibility to illness, disrepute, indecent exposure; and weakened wisdom is the sixth thing.

ru.text: (1) потеря нынешнего богатства, (2) увеличение ссор, (3) подверженность болезням, (4) приобретение порочной репутации, (5) обнажение интимных мест, (6) ослабление понимания.

rootText: Ime kho, gahapatiputta, cha ādīnavā surāmerayamajjappamādaṭṭhānānuyoge.
translationText: These are the six drawbacks of habitually consuming intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor.

ru.text: Таковы, о сын домохозяина, шесть опасностей употребления опьяняющих веществ, приводящих к беспечности.

translationText: 6. Six Drawbacks of Roaming the Streets at Night

ru.text: 6. Шесть опасностей разгуливания по улицам в неподходящее время

translationText: There are these six drawbacks of roaming the streets at night.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, таковы шесть опасностей разгуливания по улицам в неподходящее время.

rootText: Attāpissa agutto arakkhito hoti, puttadāropissa agutto arakkhito hoti, sāpateyyampissa aguttaṁ arakkhitaṁ hoti, saṅkiyo ca hoti pāpakesu ṭhānesu, abhūtavacanañca tasmiṁ rūhati, bahūnañca dukkhadhammānaṁ purakkhato hoti.
translationText: Yourself, your partners and children, and your property are all left unguarded. You’re suspected of bad deeds. Untrue rumors spread about you. You’re at the forefront of many things that entail suffering.

ru.text: (1) он сам незащищен и не охраняем, (2) его жена и дети незащищены и неохраняемы, (3) его имущество незащищено и не охраняемо, (4) его подозревают в дурных поступках, (5) про него расходятся ложные слухи, (6) он встречается со многими неприятностями.

rootText: Ime kho, gahapatiputta, cha ādīnavā vikālavisikhācariyānuyoge.
translationText: These are the six drawbacks of roaming the streets at night.

ru.text: Таковы, о сын домохозяина, шесть шесть опасностей разгуливания по улицам в неподходящее время.

translationText: 7. Six Drawbacks of Festivals

ru.text: 7. Шесть опасностей частого посещения ярмарок

translationText: There are these six drawbacks of frequenting festivals.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, таковы шесть опасностей частого посещения ярмарок.

rootText: Kva naccaṁ, kva gītaṁ, kva vāditaṁ, kva akkhānaṁ, kva pāṇissaraṁ, kva kumbhathunanti.
translationText: You’re always thinking: ‘Where’s the dancing? Where’s the singing? Where’s the music? Where are the stories? Where’s the applause? Where are the kettledrums?’

ru.text: Он спрашивает: (1) где танцы? (2) где пение? (3) где музыка? (4) где декламация? (5) где аплодисменты? (6) где бьют в барабаны?

rootText: Ime kho, gahapatiputta, cha ādīnavā samajjābhicaraṇe.
translationText: These are the six drawbacks of frequenting festivals.

ru.text: Таковы, о сын домохозяина, шесть опасностей частого посещения ярмарок.

translationText: 8. Six Drawbacks of Gambling

ru.text: 8. Шесть опасностей азартных игр, вызывающих беспечность

translationText: There are these six drawbacks of habitually gambling.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, таковы шесть опасностей пристрастия к азартным играм, вызывающим беспечность.

rootText: Jayaṁ veraṁ pasavati, jino vittamanusocati, sandiṭṭhikā dhanajāni, sabhāgatassa vacanaṁ na rūhati, mittāmaccānaṁ paribhūto hoti, āvāhavivāhakānaṁ apatthito hoti:
translationText: Victory breeds enmity. The loser mourns their money. There is immediate loss of wealth. A gambler’s word carries no weight in public assembly. Friends and colleagues treat them with contempt. And no-one wants to marry a gambler, for they think:

ru.text: (1) победитель вызывает ненависть, (2) проигравший горюет о потерянном богатстве, (3) потеря нынешнего богатства, (4) на собрании его словам не придают значения,(5) его презирают друзья и партнёры, (6) он не востребован берущими жену или отдающими в жёны, потому что люди говорят,

translationText: ‘This individual is a gambler—they’re not able to support a partner.’

ru.text: что он игрок и не может содержать жену.

rootText: Ime kho, gahapatiputta, cha ādīnavā jūtappamādaṭṭhānānuyoge.
translationText: These are the six drawbacks of habitually gambling.

ru.text: Таковы, о сын домохозяина, шесть опасностей пристрастия к азартным играм, вызывающим беспечность.

translationText: 9. Six Drawbacks of Bad Friends

ru.text: 9. Шесть опасностей связи с порочными друзьями

translationText: There are these six drawbacks of bad friends.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, таковы шесть опасностей связи с порочными друзьями.

rootText: Ye dhuttā, ye soṇḍā, ye pipāsā, ye nekatikā, ye vañcanikā, ye sāhasikā. Tyāssa mittā honti te sahāyā.
translationText: You become friends and companions with those who are addicts, carousers, drunkards, frauds, swindlers, and thugs.

ru.text: Когда любители азартных игр, люди с пристрастиями, пьяницы, жулики, мошенники, бандиты — его друзья и товарищи.

rootText: Ime kho, gahapatiputta, cha ādīnavā pāpamittānuyoge.
translationText: These are the six drawbacks of bad friends.

ru.text: Таковы, о сын домохозяина, шесть опасностей связи с порочными друзьями.

translationText: 10. Six Drawbacks of Laziness

ru.text: 10. Шесть опасностей склонности к лени

translationText: There are these six drawbacks of habitual laziness.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, таковы шесть опасностей склонности к лени.

rootText: Atisītanti kammaṁ na karoti, atiuṇhanti kammaṁ na karoti, atisāyanti kammaṁ na karoti, atipātoti kammaṁ na karoti, atichātosmīti kammaṁ na karoti, atidhātosmīti kammaṁ na karoti.
translationText: You don’t get your work done because you think: ‘It’s too cold! It’s too hot. It’s too late! It’s too early! I’m too hungry! I’m too full!’

ru.text: Он не работает, говоря: (1) что слишком холодно, (2) что слишком жарко, (3) что слишком поздно, (4) что слишком рано, (5) что он очень голоден, (6) что он очень наелся.

rootText: Tassa evaṁ kiccāpadesabahulassa viharato anuppannā ceva bhogā nuppajjanti, uppannā ca bhogā parikkhayaṁ gacchanti.
translationText: By dwelling on so many excuses for not working, you don’t make any more money, and the money you already have runs out.

ru.text: Живя таким образом, он оставляет много обязанностей невыполненными, не приобретает нового богатства, а богатство, которое он уже приобрёл, истощается.

rootText: Ime kho, gahapatiputta, cha ādīnavā ālasyānuyoge”ti.
translationText: These are the six drawbacks of habitual laziness.”

ru.text: Таковы, о сын домохозяина, шесть опасностей склонности к лени.“

translationText: That is what the Buddha said.

ru.text: Так сказал Благословенный.

rootText: Idaṁ vatvāna sugato athāparaṁ etadavoca satthā:
translationText: Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say:

ru.text: Сказав это, Достигший блага учитель добавил:

translationText: “Some are just drinking buddies,

ru.text: „Он друг по бутылке;

translationText: some call you their dear, dear friend,

ru.text: он только говорит, 'друг, друг'.

translationText: but a true friend is one

ru.text: Тот, кто при возникшей потребности

translationText: who stands by you in need.

ru.text: остаётся товарищем — тот и есть друг.

translationText: Sleeping late, adultery,

ru.text: Поздний сон, посещение чужой жены,

translationText: making enemies, harmfulness,

ru.text: проявление враждебности, нанесение вреда (бесполезность),

translationText: bad friends, and avarice:

ru.text: порочные друзья, скаредность

translationText: these six grounds ruin a person.

ru.text: — эти шесть причин разрушают человека.

translationText: With bad friends, bad comrades,

ru.text: Человек, у которого порочные товарищи и друзья,

translationText: bad behavior and alms-resort,

ru.text: проводит время в порочных поступках.

translationText: a man falls to ruin

ru.text: В этом и в следующем —

translationText: in both this world and the next.

ru.text: в обоих мирах его ждет крах.

translationText: Dice, women, toddy, song and dance;

ru.text: Кости (азартные игры), женщины, алкоголь, танцы, пение,

translationText: sleeping by day and roaming at night;

ru.text: сон днём, гуляние в неподходящие часы,

translationText: bad friends, and avarice:

ru.text: порочные товарищи, скаредность —

translationText: these six grounds ruin a person.

ru.text: эти девять причин разрушают человека.

translationText: They play dice and drink beer,

ru.text: Кто играет в кости и пьёт опьяняющие напитки,

translationText: and consort with women loved by others.

ru.text: ходит к женщинам, которые дороги другим как жизнь,

translationText: Associating with the worse, not the better,

ru.text: водится с низкими людьми, а не с почтенными —

translationText: they diminish like the waning moon.

ru.text: он убывает, как луна на ущербе.

translationText: One who is broke and destitute due to toddy,

ru.text: Кто пьян, беден, нищ,

translationText: thirsty, drinking in the bar,

ru.text: из-за жажды приходит напиться в киоск у дороги,

translationText: drowning in debt,

ru.text: тонет в долгах как камень в воде,

translationText: will quickly lose their way.

ru.text: быстро останется без родственников.

translationText: When you’re in the habit of sleeping late,

ru.text: Кто привык спать днём

translationText: seeing night as time to rise,

ru.text: и считает ночь временем просыпаться,

translationText: and always getting drunk,

ru.text: всегда пьян и нетрезв,

translationText: you can’t keep up the household life.

ru.text: не может содержать домохозяйство.

translationText: ‘Too cold, too hot,

ru.text: Кто говорит, что слишком жарко, слишком холодно,

translationText: too late,’ they say.

ru.text: слишком поздно,

translationText: When the young neglect their work like this,

ru.text: забрасывает свои дела —

translationText: riches pass them by.

ru.text: так молодёжь упускает возможности.

translationText: But one who considers heat and cold

ru.text: Но тот, кто обращает на холод или жару

translationText: as no more than blades of grass—

ru.text: не больше внимания, чем на стебельки травы,

translationText: he does his duties as a man,

ru.text: и выполняет мужскую работу,

translationText: and happiness never fails.”

ru.text: не упускает удачи“.

translationText: 11. Fake Friends

ru.text: 11. Враги в облике друзей

translationText: “Householder’s son, you should recognize these four enemies disguised as friends:

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, следующих четырёх следует определять как врагов в облике друзей:

rootText: Aññadatthuharo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo, vacīparamo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo, anuppiyabhāṇī amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo, apāyasahāyo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo.
translationText: the taker, the talker, the flatterer, the spender.

ru.text: (1) того, кто присваивает чужое имущество, следует определять как врага в облике друга; (2) того, кто помогает только на словах, следует определять как врага в облике друга; (3) того, кто льстит, следует определять как врага в облике друга; (4) товарища по растрате следует определять как врага в облике друга.

rootText: Catūhi kho, gahapatiputta, ṭhānehi aññadatthuharo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo.
translationText: You can recognize a fake friend who’s all take on four grounds.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, четырьмя способами того, кто присваивает чужое имущество, следует определять как врага в облике друга:

translationText: Your possessions end up theirs.

ru.text: (1) он присваивает чужое имущество,

translationText: Giving little, they expect a lot.

ru.text: (2) он дает немного и хочет многого,

translationText: They do their duty out of fear.

ru.text: (3) он выполняет свои обязанности из страха,

translationText: They associate for their own advantage.

ru.text: (4) он общается ради собственной выгоды.

rootText: Imehi kho, gahapatiputta, catūhi ṭhānehi aññadatthuharo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo.
translationText: You can recognize a fake friend who’s all take on these four grounds.

ru.text: Этими четырьмя способами того, кто присваивает чужое имущество, следует определять как врага в облике друга.

rootText: Catūhi kho, gahapatiputta, ṭhānehi vacīparamo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo.
translationText: You can recognize a fake friend who’s all talk on four grounds.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, четырьмя способами того, кто помогает только на словах, следует определять как врага в облике друга:

rootText: Atītena paṭisantharati, anāgatena paṭisantharati, niratthakena saṅgaṇhāti, paccuppannesu kiccesu byasanaṁ dasseti.
translationText: They’re hospitable in the past. They’re hospitable in the future. They’re full of meaningless pleasantries. When something needs doing in the present they point to their own misfortune.

ru.text: (1) он встречает прошлым, (2) он встречает будущим, (3) он принимает гостя пустыми словами, (4) когда возникают какие-то дела где требуется его помощь он указывает на собственное несчастье.

rootText: Imehi kho, gahapatiputta, catūhi ṭhānehi vacīparamo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo.
translationText: You can recognize a fake friend who’s all talk on these four grounds.

ru.text: Этими четырьмя способами, о сын домохозяина, того, кто помогает только на словах, следует определять как врага в облике друга.

rootText: Catūhi kho, gahapatiputta, ṭhānehi anuppiyabhāṇī amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo.
translationText: You can recognize a fake friend who’s a flatterer on four grounds.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, четырьмя способами того, кто льстит, следует определять как врага в облике друга:

rootText: Pāpakampissa anujānāti, kalyāṇampissa anujānāti, sammukhāssa vaṇṇaṁ bhāsati, parammukhāssa avaṇṇaṁ bhāsati.
translationText: They support you equally in doing bad and doing good. They praise you to your face, and put you down behind your back.

ru.text: (1) он одобряет плохое, (2) он неуместно одобряет хорошее, (3) он хвалит его в его присутствии, (4) он ругает его в его отсутствии.

rootText: Imehi kho, gahapatiputta, catūhi ṭhānehi anuppiyabhāṇī amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo.
translationText: You can recognize a fake friend who’s a flatterer on these four grounds.

ru.text: Этими четырьмя способами, о сын домохозяина, того, кто льстит, следует определять как врага в облике друга.

rootText: Catūhi kho, gahapatiputta, ṭhānehi apāyasahāyo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo.
translationText: You can recognize a fake friend who’s a spender on four grounds.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, четырьмя способами товарища по растрате следует определять как врага в облике друга:

rootText: Surāmerayamajjappamādaṭṭhānānuyoge sahāyo hoti, vikālavisikhācariyānuyoge sahāyo hoti, samajjābhicaraṇe sahāyo hoti, jūtappamādaṭṭhānānuyoge sahāyo hoti.
translationText: They accompany you when drinking, roaming the streets at night, frequenting festivals, and gambling.

ru.text: (1) он товарищ в пристрастии к опьяняющим веществам, которые вызывают беспечность, (2) он товарищ в разгуливании по улицам в неподходящее время, (3) он товарищ в частом посещении ярмарок, (4) он товарищ в пристрастии к азартным играми, которые вызывают беспечность.

rootText: Imehi kho, gahapatiputta, catūhi ṭhānehi apāyasahāyo amitto mittapatirūpako veditabbo”ti.
translationText: You can recognize a fake friend who’s a spender on these four grounds.”

ru.text: Этими четырьмя способами, о сын домохозяина, товарища по растрате следует определять как врага в облике друга.»

translationText: That is what the Buddha said.

ru.text: Так сказал Благословенный.

rootText: Idaṁ vatvāna sugato athāparaṁ etadavoca satthā:
translationText: Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say:

ru.text: Сказав это, Достигший блага учитель добавил:

translationText: “One friend is all take,

ru.text: «Друг, который присваивает чужое имущество,

translationText: another all talk;

ru.text: друг, который помогает только на словах,

translationText: one’s just a flatterer,

ru.text: Друг, который льстит,

translationText: and one’s a comrade who spends.

ru.text: товарищ по растрате,

translationText: An astute person understands

ru.text: Эти четверо — недруги.

translationText: these four enemies for what they are

ru.text: Мудрый человек, зная об этом,

translationText: and keeps them at a distance,

ru.text: держится от них на расстоянии,

translationText: as they’d shun a risky road.”

ru.text: как от опасной дороги.

translationText: 12. Good-Hearted Friends

ru.text: 12. Добросердечные друзья

translationText: “Householder’s son, you should recognize these four good-hearted friends:

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, этих четырёх нужно определять как добросердечных друзей:

rootText: Upakāro mitto suhado veditabbo, samānasukhadukkho mitto suhado veditabbo, atthakkhāyī mitto suhado veditabbo, anukampako mitto suhado veditabbo.
translationText: the helper, the friend in good times and bad, the counselor, and the one who’s sympathetic.

ru.text: того, (1) кто помогает, следует определять как добросердечного друга, (2) кто остаётся таким же в счастье и в горе следует определять как добросердечного друга, (3) кто объясняет, что вам полезно, следует определять как добросердечного друга, (4) кто сочувствует следует определять как добросердечного друга.

rootText: Catūhi kho, gahapatiputta, ṭhānehi upakāro mitto suhado veditabbo.
translationText: You can recognize a good-hearted friend who’s a helper on four grounds.

ru.text: О сын домохозяина, четырьмя способами того, кто помогает, следует определять как добросердечного друга:

rootText: Pamattaṁ rakkhati, pamattassa sāpateyyaṁ rakkhati, bhītassa saraṇaṁ hoti, uppannesu kiccakaraṇīyesu taddiguṇaṁ bhogaṁ anuppadeti.
translationText: They guard you when you’re negligent. They guard your property when you’re negligent. They keep you safe in times of danger. When something needs doing, they provide you with twice the money you need.

ru.text: (1) он защищает вас, когда вы в беспечном состоянии, (2) он защищает ваше имущество, когда вы в беспечном состоянии, (3) он становится прибежищем, когда вы испуганы, (4) в возникших делах когда что-то нужно даёт в два раза больше.
